one_line_takeaway: Making the simulated multi-turn conversational trajectory itself -- not a single prompt -- the object of evolutionary search, guided by two new mutators (troubleshooting, mechanistic) and graded judge feedback, achieves near-total jailbreak success on open-source LLMs and transfers 70-90% of maximal-harm attacks to GPT/Gemini frontier models without adaptation, while Claude models resist the same attacks at 15-17.5%.
key_finding: ContextualJailbreak reaches 100% ASR@4 on gpt-oss:20B, qwen3-8B, and llama3.1:70B, and 90% ASR@4 / 72% ASR@5 on gpt-oss:120B, beating the four baselines by 31-96 percentage points on average; notably gpt-oss:120B has 0% ASR@4 under DirectRequest yet 72% ASR@5 under contextual priming. The controlled RQ3 ablation isolating structure from content (Table 3) shows a strict hierarchy Context Priming >> Single Sequence >> Direct: mean ASR@4/ASR@5 across the four targets is 37.5%/9.0% (Direct), 73.5%/29.0% (Single Sequence, same content flattened into one message), and 97.5%/90.0% (Context Priming) -- i.e. structuring the same semantic content as alternating turns adds ~61 points of ASR@5 beyond what the content alone buys. Mutator-level discovery counts (Table 4) show troubleshooting is the dominant early-to-mid-search driver (22/23/27/43 first-discoveries at ASR@1-4) while mechanistic is strongest at ASR@3-4 (14, 41), and expand is disproportionately a 'finishing' mutator with the most ASR@5 discoveries (46) despite weaker mid-search contribution; the two novel mutators (troubleshooting, mechanistic) are reported as the strongest drivers into high-ASR regions on the hardest targets. Intra-matrix transfer (Table 5) is directional and non-monotonic in model size: templates from 70B reach 92.0% ASR@4 on 8B but only 6.0% ASR@4 on 120B; templates from 8B transfer poorly to 20B (16.0%) but well to 70B (64.0%). The headline frontier-transfer result (Table 6): the 40 maximal-harm (score-5) attacks found against gpt-oss:120B, replayed verbatim with no adaptation, reach 90.0% ASR@4 / 27.5% ASR@5 on gpt-4o-mini, 70.0%/17.5% on gpt-5, and 70.0%/22.5% on gemini-3-flash, versus only 17.5%/7.5% on claude-opus-4-7 and 15.0%/5.0% on claude-sonnet-4-6 (26/40 and 25/40 pure refusals respectively, versus 0-4 pure refusals on the OpenAI/Google targets). The paper reads this as evidence that alignment recipe, not model scale, drives robustness (llama3.1:70B is more vulnerable on average than the smaller gpt-oss:20B; scaling Claude Sonnet to Opus yields no appreciable transfer-robustness gain).
core_contribution: ContextualJailbreak, a black-box automated red-teaming strategy that performs evolutionary search over a simulated multi-turn 'primed dialogue' (a fabricated conversational history ending in a harmful request), rather than optimizing a single-turn prompt string as prior optimization-based jailbreak methods (PAIR, TAP, GPTFuzz, Papillon, AutoDAN, AutoDAN-Turbo) do. The generator conditions on a score-biased sample of prior templates (FunSearch-style softmax cluster selection) and one of five semantically defined mutation operators: roleplay, scenario, expand (adapted from Papillon-style transformations), and two operators introduced as novel contributions of this paper, troubleshooting (reframes the goal as diagnosing a failed process) and mechanistic (analytical causal decomposition). Candidate multi-turn templates are instantiated with a target harmful behavior, sent to the target LLM as a single API call, and scored by a two-stage judge: a lightweight barrier judge that filters clear refusals, then a main judge that assigns a graded 0-5 harm score (Hagendorff et al. rubric) plus disclaimer/refusal flags; scored templates re-enter the seed pool, closing the evolutionary loop. The whole system, baselines, targets, and evaluation pipeline run inside IBM's ARES red-teaming framework.
threat_model: Black-box adversary consistent with prior optimization-based red-teaming (PAIR/Papillon/TAP threat models): can query the target LLM via API or local endpoint (one query = one multi-turn input, finite attempt budget B), can draw harmful goals from public benchmarks (HarmBench) with no seed jailbreak templates required, and controls two auxiliary LLMs it does not target -- a generator/mutator model and a judge model. Constraints: no logits/parameters/activations from the target, no knowledge of the target's training/RLHF/alignment recipe, no knowledge of deployed defenses (filters, guards, hidden system prompts). In the frontier-transfer regime the adversary additionally cannot adapt attacks per-target: templates discovered against an open-source proxy are replayed verbatim against closed APIs. Objective: craft, per harmful behavior, a single multi-turn input that makes the target produce a substantive (non-refusal) response scored >=4 (near-complete, ASR@4) or =5 (fully harmful, ASR@5) on the judge's 0-5 rubric.
limitations: The paper's own stated limitations: (1) the judge is a single fixed LLM (gpt-oss:120B) used consistently across all methods, which the authors argue makes ASR comparisons internally consistent but note that systematic judge bias is not eliminated and multi-judge agreement / human adjudication / evaluator-backdoor auditing (cf. BadJudge) are left to future work; (2) only 50 of 400 HarmBench behaviors are evaluated per condition (a coreset selection, justified via submodular coverage but still a subsample, chosen for compute-cost reasons -- full-set evaluation would take 'several days of continuous GPU computation per condition'); (3) frontier-transfer experiments test only 5 closed models and reuse the 40 templates that scored maximally against one specific open-source proxy (gpt-oss:120B), so the observed OpenAI/Google-vs-Anthropic asymmetry is a single-proxy, single-run finding, not adaptively re-attacked against each frontier model; (4) the complete adversarial template database is withheld from public release (shared only with providers under responsible disclosure) for dual-use reasons, limiting independent reproducibility of the strongest attacks; (5) the paper explicitly frames the Anthropic-vs-OpenAI/Google asymmetry as 'under this attack model' / 'this attack model' -- i.e. as evidence of relative robustness to this specific priming strategy, not a general robustness ranking.

datasets_used: HarmBench (400 harmful behaviors; a stratified 50-behavior coreset subset of 278 behaviors across 5 categories used for all main experiments)
quantitative_results: ContextualJailbreak ASR@4: 100% on gpt-oss:20B, 100% on qwen3-8B, 100% on llama3.1:70B, 90% on gpt-oss:120B
Outperforms 4 baselines by 31-96 percentage points on average (Figure 2)
Direct vs Single-Sequence vs Context-Priming mean ASR@4/ASR@5 across 4 models: 37.5%/9.0% vs 73.5%/29.0% vs 97.5%/90.0% (Table 3)
Mutator ASR-threshold discovery counts (Table 4): troubleshooting 22/23/27/43/35 at ASR@1-5; mechanistic 10/9/14/41/36; expand 19/12/20/21/46; scenario 10/14/10/26/39; roleplay 6/7/12/22/27
5-mutator config final performance on gpt-oss:20B: 100% ASR@4, 88% ASR@5 (ablation study, Section 5.6)

Frontier transfer of the 40 gpt-oss:120B maximal-score attacks (Table 6, verbatim replay, no adaptation): gpt-4o-mini 90.0% ASR@4 / 27.5% ASR@5; gpt-5 70.0% / 17.5%; gemini-3-flash 70.0% / 22.5%; claude-opus-4-7 17.5% / 7.5%; claude-sonnet-4-6 15.0% / 5.0%
Pure refusals (harm=0) out of 40 transferred attacks: 26 on claude-opus-4-7, 25 on claude-sonnet-4-6, versus 0-4 on gpt-4o-mini/gpt-5/gemini-3-flash
Budget analysis (Table 2): gpt-oss:120B ASR@4 rises 40% (B=10) -> 74% (B=50) -> 90% (B=100); qwen3-8B already 100% ASR@4 at B=10
Coreset selection: greedy Facility Location coverage 0.629 (+11.1% over random), worst-case coverage 0.246 (best among 10 methods), within 99.6% of ILP optimum (0.631)
baselines_compared: Crescendo (hand-designed multi-turn escalation attack, no in-loop search)
Ablation baselines within RQ3: Direct and Single-Sequence (flattened) input formats using identical semantic content as Context Priming

Subset-selection comparison (Appendix F, not a security baseline): random sampling, k-means+medoid, k-medoids (FasterPAM), Facility Location via Apricot, k-center, kernel herding (MMD), DPP greedy MAP, Graph Cut, exact ILP
